name: Playa de El Espartal in Salinas to Playa de El Puerto via the Senda Norte
name_original: Playa de El Espartal en Salinas a Playa de El Puerto por la Senda Norte
region_intro: Jogging routes around Castrillón offer a diverse landscape for runners, characterized by its extensive coastline, sandy beaches, and gentle coastal paths. The region features routes that traverse urban areas, connect coastal towns, and provide views of the Bay of Biscay. Elevation changes are generally moderate, making many trails accessible for various fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Castrillón The most popular jogging route is Playa de El Espartal in Salinas to Playa de El Puerto via the Senda Norte, a 5.4 miles (8.7 km) trail that takes just under 1 hour to complete. This route follows a scenic coastal path with views of the beaches. Another top favourite among local runners is Plaza de España, Avilés – Niemeyer Center ️ Avilés loop from Salinas, a difficult 11.7 miles (18.8 km) path. This route combines urban exploration with coastal stretches, connecting Salinas with Avilés. Local runners also love the Pinos Altos – Arnao Tunnel loop from Salinas, a 7.2 miles (11.6 km) trail leading through coastal areas and the Arnao Tunnel, often completed in about 1 hour 12 minutes. Jogging around Castrillón is defined by coastal paths, sandy beaches, and gentle hills, offering a variety of running experiences. The network provides options for different ability levels, from easy jogging routes to more challenging long distance jogging trails. The routes in Castrillón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.25 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 600 runners have used komoot to explore Castrillón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Castrillón?
A: Castrillón offers a wide variety of running experiences, with over 50 routes available on komoot. These range from easy coastal paths to more challenging long-distance trails, catering to all fitness levels.

Q: Are there easy running routes for beginners in Castrillón?
A: Yes, Castrillón has several easy running routes suitable for beginners. There are 4 routes specifically categorized as easy, often featuring gentle coastal paths and minimal elevation changes, perfect for a relaxed run.

Q: Are there long-distance running trails in Castrillón?
A: For those seeking a longer challenge, Castrillón provides 13 difficult running trails. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10513741" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de España, Avilés – Niemeyer Center 🖼️ Avilés loop from Salinas&lt;/a&gt;, which is 11.7 miles (18.8 km) long and combines urban exploration with coastal stretches.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about running in Castrillón?
A: The running routes in Castrillón are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.25 stars from over 4 reviews. Runners particularly appreciate the diverse landscapes, including extensive coastlines, sandy beaches, and scenic views of the Bay of Biscay.

Q: Are there circular running routes in Castrillón?
A: Yes, many running routes in Castrillón are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31946316" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pinos Altos – Arnao Tunnel loop from Salinas&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 7.2 miles (11.6 km) circular trail that takes you through coastal areas and the historic Arnao Tunnel.

Q: Can I find family-friendly running paths in Castrillón?
A: Castrillón's gentle coastal paths and moderate elevation changes make many routes suitable for families. While specific 'family-friendly' designations aren't always present, routes with lower difficulty grades and shorter distances, such as those along the beaches, are generally good options for running with children.

Q: Are the running trails in Castrillón dog-friendly?
A: Many of Castrillón's outdoor areas and coastal paths are generally dog-friendly, especially outside of peak season on certain beaches. It's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash and check local regulations for specific areas or beaches before heading out.

Q: Are there scenic running routes with viewpoints in Castrillón?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its stunning coastal scenery. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056048226/playa-de-el-espartal-in-salinas-to-playa-de-el-puerto-via-the-senda-norte" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de El Espartal in Salinas to Playa de El Puerto via the Senda Norte&lt;/a&gt; offer continuous views of the beaches and the Bay of Biscay.

Q: What can I see along the running routes in Castrillón?
A: Beyond the natural beauty of the coastline, you can encounter several interesting landmarks. For instance, you might pass by historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353344" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Selgas Palace and the Jesús Nazareno Church‑Pantheon&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353340" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de San Martín&lt;/a&gt;, adding cultural interest to your run.

Q: What about parking for running routes in Castrillón?
A: Parking is generally available near popular starting points for many running routes, especially in coastal towns like Salinas and Piedras Blancas. Look for designated parking areas near beaches or urban centers, though availability can vary during peak seasons.

Q: Can I access running routes in Castrillón using public transport?
A: Yes, many areas in Castrillón are well-connected by public transport, particularly buses, which can take you to towns and coastal areas where running routes begin. This allows for flexible planning, especially for linear routes or if you prefer not to drive.

Q: Are there options for winter running in Castrillón?
A: Castrillón's mild coastal climate makes it suitable for winter running, though conditions can be cooler and wetter. The coastal paths remain accessible, and the lower tourist numbers can offer a more tranquil experience. Always check local weather forecasts and trail conditions before heading out.
tips: Located inside the beautiful Museo de las Anclas (open-air museum). In memory of the late French oceanographer. Before its installation, it was exhibited in the Picasso room of the Unesco headquarters in Paris.

This tunnel has connected Salinas and Arnao since the mid-19th century. It was built for the railway that took the coal from the Arnao mine to the port of Avilés. After the closure of the mine, trucks from Asturiana de Zinc and the urban bus continue to pass through it daily.
The views of the Cantabrian Sea from the Asturian coast are unforgettable. On clear days, you can see as far as the Picos de Europa.

waypoints.name: Views of the Bay of Biscay
